narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate studies where you earned a solid 3.10 GPA and scored 31 on the LSAT, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' bar exam outcomes, predicting your likelihood of failing the bar on the first try at 45.8%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes those with lower predicted failure risks to ensure strong bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong academic performance. Your Year 1 decile of 1 also contributed somewhat toward this concern, though less strongly, as it similarly falls well below average. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest favorable influence, pulling the prediction toward success since your 3.10 exceeds what many peers achieve. Your LSAT score and family income bracket each provided meaningful boosts too, with your score aligning decently against the higher averages and your upper-middle income offering a stability edge over lower brackets. Compared to typical students, your full-time status is the norm and didn't sway things much either way. While these academic rankings stood out as the top drivers compared to your other solid attributes, the model weighed them heavily in light of historical data where low deciles often correlate with bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps prepping more intensively for the LSAT if feasible, and reapplying with updated credentials that highlight your strengths like that GPA. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas, and we're rooting for your next steps. Keep pushing—you've got a strong foundation to build on.
